skill.name: Azure.Messaging.EventHubs (.NET)
skill.description: This skill provides guidance and code assistance for working with Azure Event Hubs in .NET applications using the Azure.Messaging.EventHubs library. It helps developers implement event streaming, publish-subscribe patterns, and real-time data processing solutions.
skill.summary: Assists with Azure Event Hubs .NET SDK for event streaming and pub-sub patterns
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This is a prompt-only skill with no executable code. The static analyzer scanned 0 files (0 lines) and detected no security issues. The skill provides guidance on Azure Event Hubs .NET SDK usage through prompts and documentation. No dangerous patterns, network requests, filesystem access, environment variable usage, or external command execution were detected.

content.user_title: Build Event Streaming with Azure Event Hubs
content.value_statement: This skill helps developers integrate Azure Event Hubs into .NET applications for real-time event streaming, enabling scalable pub-sub messaging and event-driven architectures.
actual_capabilities: Generate producer code for publishing events to Event Hubs
Generate consumer code for processing events from Event Hubs
Explain Event Hubs concepts including partitions, consumer groups, and checkpoints
Provide guidance on connection string management and authentication
Assist with event batch processing and throughput optimization
Help troubleshoot common Event Hubs issues and errors
limitations: Does not provide live Azure account or Event Hubs namespace
Cannot execute actual network requests to Azure services
Does not manage or provision Azure resources
Generated code samples require valid Azure subscription and Event Hubs instance

use_cases.title: Build event-driven microservices
use_cases.description: Create loosely coupled microservices that communicate through event streaming for scalable distributed systems

use_cases.title: Implement real-time data pipelines
use_cases.description: Process streaming data from IoT devices, click streams, or log aggregation in real-time

use_cases.title: Modernize legacy messaging systems
use_cases.description: Migrate from traditional message queues to event streaming with Event Hubs integration

prompt_templates.title: Basic event producer
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to create an Event Hubs producer in .NET to send messages to a specific hub.
prompt_templates.scenario: Starting new project with Event Hubs integration

prompt_templates.title: Event consumer with checkpointing
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an Event Hubs consumer that processes events and saves checkpoints for reliable processing.

prompt_templates.title: Batch event publishing
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate code to publish events in batches for better throughput and performance.

prompt_templates.title: Partition routing strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain how to implement a partition routing strategy for ordered event processing.
prompt_templates.scenario: Implementing ordered event processing across multiple partitions

output_examples.input: Show me how to create an Event Hubs producer in .NET
output_examples.output: To create an Event Hubs producer in .NET, use the EventHubProducerClient from the Azure.Messaging.EventHubs package. First, install the NuGet package, then create a producer instance with your connection string and hub name. The producer can send single events or batches of events. Remember to dispose of the producer when done or use 'using' statements for automatic cleanup.

output_examples.input: Explain partitions and consumer groups in Event Hubs
output_examples.output: Partitions are the basic unit of parallelism in Event Hubs. Each partition holds a sequence of events and can be processed independently. Consumer groups are logical subscribers that maintain their own position in the stream. Multiple consumer groups enable different consumers to read the same events independently. For ordered processing, all related events should go to the same partition using a partition key.

best_practices: Use partition keys for related events to maintain ordering within a partition
Implement checkpointing in consumers to enable resume after failures
Use batch publishing for high-throughput scenarios to reduce network overhead
anti_patterns: Do not create a new producer for each message - reuse producer instances
Avoid processing events synchronously in the receive callback - use async processing
Do not ignore the PartitionContext when implementing checkpointing for reliable processing

faq.question: What is Azure Event Hubs?
faq.answer: Azure Event Hubs is a fully managed real-time event streaming service from Microsoft Azure. It can ingest millions of events per second from various sources and deliver them to multiple consumers.

faq.question: Do I need an Azure account to use this skill?
faq.answer: This skill provides code guidance and examples. To run the generated code, you need an Azure subscription and an Event Hubs namespace with an event hub.

faq.question: What is the difference between partitions and consumer groups?
faq.answer: Partitions are physical divisions of the event stream that enable parallel processing. Consumer groups are logical views that allow multiple independent consumers to read the same stream at different positions.

faq.question: How do I handle event ordering?
faq.answer: Events within a single partition are ordered. To maintain ordering across related events, use a partition key when sending events to ensure they go to the same partition.

faq.question: What is checkpointing?
faq.answer: Checkpointing is the process of saving the position of processed events so that if the consumer restarts, it can resume from where it left off rather than reprocessing all events.

faq.question: Can this skill help with Azure IoT Hub integration?
faq.answer: Yes, Azure IoT Hub can stream events to Event Hubs using built-in endpoints. This skill can help you create consumers that process these events from the IoT Hub-compatible endpoint.
